text: The surgeon uses four 8mm trocars to access the abdominal cavity, visually assessing the diseased sigmoid colon and marking the proximal margin for resection.
The surgeon begins the dissection of the sigmoid colon in a lateral to medial fashion, deviating from the usual medial to lateral approach.
The surgeon begins preserving the superior hemorrhoidal artery during the robotic sigmoid resection, carefully dissecting around the vessel to maintain its integrity.
The surgeon performs a limited lateral dissection to unfold the sigmoid mesentery, with the ureter visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon has fully mobilized the left colon and splenic flexure. They are preserving the superior hemorrhoidal artery while dissecting through the mesentery, carefully assessing the proximal and distal margins.
The surgeon creates a window in the mesentery, facilitating access to the proximal and distal margins. Careful attention is paid to the marked margins to avoid excessive removal of the mesentery.
The surgeon takes down the mesentery completely, cleaning it off the back of the bowel wall, and proceeds to dissect down to the rectosigmoid junction, marking the area for reference.
The surgeon uses cautery to mark the previously chosen margin on the mesentery after checking the ICG fluorescence, ensuring clarity for the upcoming transection under white light.
The surgeon marks the distal margin on the bowel wall using ICG fluorescence and proceeds to transect the bowel with scissors, opting not to use staplers for this portion of the colectomy.

The surgeon divides the proximal bowel, encountering diverticulum during the process. The specimen is morcellated into seven pieces, which are individually extracted through the rectum by the surgeon.
The surgeon removes the last piece of the specimen and cleans the surgical field, ensuring all spilled waste is cleared from the area.

The surgeon performs a single-layer anastomosis, starting with a posterior silk suture to align the bowel. The patient is positioned in mild Trendelenburg to reduce tension on the proximal bowel. The surgeon identifies a diverticulum on the right side of the proximal bowel, removes it, and tucks it inside the bowel to prevent involvement in the anastomosis. The anastomosis is sewn from the center out to the left side, addressing the size mismatch between the proximal bowel and the larger rectum.

The surgeon takes full-thickness bites with the suture needle, advancing further on the rectal side than the proximal bowel side to accommodate the size mismatch during the hand-sewn end-to-end anastomosis. The needle is parked temporarily as the surgeon starts sewing from the center towards the other side, ensuring the suture line imbricates any potential ticks at the anastomosis site.
The surgeon begins sewing the canal anteriorly to fully imbricate the anterior rail of the bowel. The area where the tick was previously removed is visible, aiding in the creation of the anastomosis.

The surgeon adjusts the suture line on the proximal bowel side to address the size discrepancy between the proximal bowel and the rectum. The suture line is advanced further on the rectal side, achieving a smooth imbrication as the surgeon works towards meeting in the middle.
The surgeon addresses a tear in the serosa, likely caused during extraction, by excising a segment of the anterior bowel wall and tucking it inside the bowel for later passage.

The surgeon observes that the anastomosis is imbricating well, switching between stitches to ensure proper alignment without planning a second layer. The ends are tied together, and any potential gaps are addressed with additional sutures. A leak test is performed by clamping the proximal bowel and flooding the pelvis. The scope is driven through the widely patent anastomosis, and the surgeon is satisfied with the result. No drains are left, and omentum is placed in the pelvis.
